1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |

63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |

70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	
47	
48	\leanok
49	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
50	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
52	  % NOTE (iter-022): CLOSED, axiom-clean {propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}.
53	  % The Lean decl is single-universe `(A B M : Type u)` (was `Type*`): L5 and
54	  % `free_localizationAway_of_away_tower` are single-universe, and the dévissage
55	  % applies them to `C = B ⧸ 𝔭`, forcing A, B into one universe. Faithful for the
56	  % geometric use (scheme patches are single-universe).
57	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
58	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
59	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
60	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
61	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
62	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
63	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
64	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
65	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
66	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
67	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
68	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
69	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
70	
71	\end{theorem}
72

102	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
103	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
104	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
105	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
106	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).

109	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
110	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
111	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
112	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
113	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
114	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route

118	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
119	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
120	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
121	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
122	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
123	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to

128	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
129	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).

132	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
133	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
134	
135	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
136	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
137	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
138	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
139	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
140	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
141	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
142	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).

144	\begin{lemma}\leanok
145	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
146	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}

148	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
149	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
150	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
151	\end{lemma}
152	
153	\begin{proof}
154	
155	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
156	  finitely presented, so
157	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
158	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
159	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
160	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
161	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
162	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
163	  \(f := r\).
164	\end{proof}

178	[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
179	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
181	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
182	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
183	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
184	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
185	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
186	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
187	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
188	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving

191	  induction).}
192	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
193	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
194	  \(A_f\).

197	\begin{proof}
198	
199	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
200	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
201	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
202	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
203	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
204	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
205	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
206	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
207	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).

210	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
211	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
212	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
213	  \mathlibok
214	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
215	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
216	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
217	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
218	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
219	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
220	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
221	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
222	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
223	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
224	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
225	\end{lemma}

249	[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
250	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
252	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
253	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
254	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	\begin{proof}
258	
259	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
260	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
261	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
262	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
263	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
264	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
265	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
266	\end{proof}

270	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}

281	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
282	\end{proof}

451	  normalisation with denominators).}
452	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
453	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
454	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
455	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
456	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
457	  \[
458	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
459	  \]
460	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
461	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
462	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
463	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
464	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).

467	\begin{proof}
468	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
469	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
470	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
471	  % NOTE: the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a Finset-fold over the finite
472	  % generating set sigma, but each fold step is the Mathlib collapsing lemma
473	  % IsIntegral.exists_multiple_integral_of_isLocalization applied to the generator AS A
474	  % WHOLE (not to its individual coefficient polynomials via gf_clear_one_denominator).
475	  % For each generator x, that lemma yields a single a_x in A^0 (nonzero, since A is a
476	  % domain) with C(a_x) * (1 (x) x) integral over MvPolynomial A; set g1 := prod_x a_x and
477	  % g := g0 * g1. The AlgHom assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
478	
479	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
480	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
481	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
482	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
483	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
484	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
485	
486	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
487	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
488	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
489	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
490	  \(x \in \sigma\) apply the Mathlib collapsing lemma
491	  \texttt{IsIntegral.exists\_multiple\_integral\_of\_isLocalization} (with
492	  \(R = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,A\), \(R_m = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,K\), \(S = B_K\), and the localizing
493	  submonoid the image of \(A^{0}\)): this returns a single
494	  \(a_x \in A^{0}\) (non-zero, as \(A\) is a domain) such that \(C(a_x)\cdot(1\otimes x)\)
495	  is integral over \(\mathrm{MvPoly}\,A = A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
496	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
497	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
498	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
499	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
500	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
501	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the landed proof discharges a whole generator
502	  at once via the collapsing lemma, which folds that primitive over the coefficients
503	  internally.)
504	
505	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
506	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
507	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
508	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
509	
510	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
511	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
512	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
513	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
514	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
515	  \[
516	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
517	  \]
518	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
519	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
520	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
521	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
522	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
523	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
524	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
525	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
526	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
527	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
528	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
529	
530	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
531	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
532	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
533	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
534	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
535	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
536	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
537	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
538	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
539	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
540	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
541	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
542	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
543	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
544	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
545	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
546	
547	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
548	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
549	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
550	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
551	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
552	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
553	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
554	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
555	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
556	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
557	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
558	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
559	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
560	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
561	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
562	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
563	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
564	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
565	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
566	\end{proof}

568	\begin{lemma}\leanok
569	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
570	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
571	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
572	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
573	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
574	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
575	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
576	\end{lemma}
577	\begin{proof}
578	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
579	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
580	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
581	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
582	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
583	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
584	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
585	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
586	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
587	\end{proof}

620	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
621	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
622	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
623	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
624	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
625	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
626	  \[
627	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
628	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
629	  \]

632	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
633	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
634	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
635	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
636	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.

639	\begin{proof}
640	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
641	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
642	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
643	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
644	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
645	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
646	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
647	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
648	
649	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
650	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
651	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
652	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
653	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
654	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
655	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
656	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
657	
658	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
659	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
660	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
661	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
662	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
663	\end{proof}

735	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
736	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
737	
738	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
739	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
740	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
741	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
742	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
743	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
744	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
745	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
746	
747	\begin{definition}\leanok
748	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
749	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
751	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
752	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
753	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
754	  \[
755	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
756	  \]
757	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
758	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
759	\end{definition}
760	
761	\begin{lemma}\leanok
762	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
763	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
764	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
765	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
766	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
767	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
768	\end{lemma}
769	\begin{proof}
770	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
771	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
772	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
773	\end{proof}
774	
775	\begin{definition}\leanok
776	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
777	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
778	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
779	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
780	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
781	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
782	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
783	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
784	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
785	\end{definition}
786	
787	\begin{lemma}\leanok
788	[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
789	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
790	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
791	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
792	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
793	\end{lemma}
794	\begin{proof}
795	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
796	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
797	\end{proof}
798	
799	\begin{lemma}\leanok
800	[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
801	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
802	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
803	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
804	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
805	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
806	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
807	\end{lemma}
808	\begin{proof}
809	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
810	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
811	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
812	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
813	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
814	\end{proof}
815	
816	\begin{lemma}\leanok
817	[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
818	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
819	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
820	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
821	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
822	  transformed monomial is the radix value
823	  \[
824	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
825	  \]
826	\end{lemma}
827	\begin{proof}
828	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
829	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
830	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
831	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
832	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
833	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
834	\end{proof}
835	
836	\begin{lemma}\leanok
837	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
838	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
839	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
840	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
841	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
842	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
843	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
844	\end{lemma}
845	\begin{proof}
846	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
847	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
848	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
849	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
850	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
851	\end{proof}
852	
853	\begin{lemma}\leanok
854	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
855	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
856	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
857	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
858	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
859	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
860	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
861	\end{lemma}
862	\begin{proof}
863	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
864	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
865	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
866	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
867	\end{proof}
868	
869	\begin{lemma}\leanok
870	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
871	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
872	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
873	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
874	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
875	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
876	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
877	\end{lemma}
878	\begin{proof}
879	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
880	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
881	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
882	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
883	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
884	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
885	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
886	\end{proof}
887	
888	\begin{lemma}\leanok
889	[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
890	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
891	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	% This chapter blueprints the Mathlib-absent infrastructure that the section
5	% graded ring R(X_s,L_s)=⊕_m Γ(X_s,L_s^{⊗m}) and section graded module
6	% M(X_s,F_s,L_s)=⊕_m Γ(X_s,F_s⊗L_s^{⊗m}) of Picard_QuotScheme.tex
7	% (def:sectionGradedRing / def:sectionGradedModule) depend on. Those two
8	% definitions are NOT restated here; this chapter supplies the three separable
9	% infrastructure layers they \uses{}. The target Lean file is
10	% AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/SectionGradedRing.lean (1:1 slug, to be scaffolded).
11	
12	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
13	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
14	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
15	
16	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
17	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
18	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
19	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
20	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
21	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
22	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
23	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
24	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
25	Mathlib counterpart at the pinned commit and must be built here, each in its own
26	section:
27	
28	\begin{enumerate}
29	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
30	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
31	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
32	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
33	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
34	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
35	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
36	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
37	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
38	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
39	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
40	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
41	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
42	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
43	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
44	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
45	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
46	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
47	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
48	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
49	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
50	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
51	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
52	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
53	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
54	    \emph{module} structure on
55	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	
58	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
59	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
60	
61	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
62	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
63	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
64	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
65	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
66	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
67	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
68	monoidal structure applies.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
71	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
72	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
73	  \mathlibok
74	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
75	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
76	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
77	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
78	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
79	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
80	  and an object \(U\),
81	  \[
82	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
83	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
84	  \]
85	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
86	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
87	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
88	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
89	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
90	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
94	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
95	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
96	  \mathlibok
97	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
98	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
99	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
100	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
101	  \[
102	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
103	  \]
104	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
105	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
106	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
107	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
108	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
109	\end{lemma}
110	
111	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
112	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
113	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
114	  \mathlibok
115	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
116	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
117	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
118	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
119	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
120	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
121	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
122	  the section graded ring.
123	\end{lemma}

126	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
127	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
128	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
129	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
130	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}

132	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
133	  % L2282-L2297).

135	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
136	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
137	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}

139	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
140	  %  $$
141	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the

172	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
173	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}

208	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
209	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
211	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}

226	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
227	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
228	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
229	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
230	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
231	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
232	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
233	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
234	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
235	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
236	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
237	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
238	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
239	  %  (formulation omitted)."
240	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
241	  power isomorphisms).}
242	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
243	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
244	  \[
245	    \mu_{m,m'} :
246	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
247	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
248	  \]
249	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
250	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
251	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
252	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
253	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
254	\end{lemma}
255	
256	\begin{proof}
257	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj,
258	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
259	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
260	  We do \emph{not} appeal to a monoidal structure on the whole category
261	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is available --- but only to the comparison
262	  on tensor \emph{powers} of the single invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\). On such
263	  locally free factors the construction is elementary, and right-exactness of the
264	  tensor product is never invoked.
265	
266	  \textbf{A local-iso criterion.} A morphism of sheaves of
267	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is an isomorphism as soon as it is one locally, i.e.\
268	  after restriction to each member of an open cover of \(X\): two-sided inverses
269	  defined over the members of a cover and agreeing on overlaps glue to a global
270	  inverse. We invoke this criterion \emph{only} on tensor products of sheaves
271	  that are locally free of rank one. On a trivialising open \(U\) where
272	  \(\mathcal{L}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), every tensor power restricts to
273	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes k}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) as well; the tensor product
274	  presheaf there takes the value \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
275	  \mathcal{O}_X(U) = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\); and the sheafification unit \(\eta\)
276	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) restricts over \(U\) to the
277	  identity. Hence over each such \(U\) the structural comparison maps used below
278	  --- the unitor, associator, and braiding of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
279	  descended objectwise from \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} --- are
280	  visibly bijective, and the criterion promotes them to genuine isomorphisms in
281	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). The subtlety that obstructs a general monoidal
282	  descent --- that the sheafification comparison
283	  \(\eta_{\mathcal P} \otimes \mathrm{id}\) need not be locally injective when
284	  \(\mathcal P\) is arbitrary, the tensor product being only right exact --- does
285	  not bite here: that object is never among the ones we test. The only maps we
286	  test are comparisons between locally free factors, where the unit is locally an
287	  identity and the comparison is a genuine local isomorphism.
288	
289	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
290	  \(m = 0\),
291	  \[
292	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
293	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
294	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
295	  \]
296	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, an isomorphism by the
297	  criterion. For the inductive step the recursive definition
298	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
299	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
300	  \[
301	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
302	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
303	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
304	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
305	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
306	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
307	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
308	  \]
309	  using first the associator and then the braiding of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
310	  each an isomorphism by the criterion. A second associator and the inductive
311	  comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) then identify the
312	  right-hand side with
313	  \[
314	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
315	      \mathcal{L}
316	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \otimes \mathrm{id}\ }
317	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
318	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m' + 1)},
319	  \]
320	  the final equality being once more the recursive definition. The composite is
321	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
322	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
323	
324	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} Every map appearing in
325	  the construction is a structural comparison isomorphism between locally free
326	  factors, so the required coherences are equalities of morphisms that may be
327	  checked locally on a common trivialising cover. Over such an open every sheaf
328	  in sight becomes \(\mathcal{O}_U\) and every comparison map becomes the
329	  identity, whence both sides of the commutativity square (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus
330	  \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and of the associativity
331	  diagram (the two bracketings of
332	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
333	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) reduce to the
334	  same local identity. Gluing the local equalities yields the global cocycle
335	  relations: the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\) is coherent in the
336	  pentagon/hexagon sense.
337	
338	  \textbf{Remark on the principled route.} One could instead obtain \(\mu\) from
339	  a genuine monoidal structure on \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) descended from the
340	  presheaf-level monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib})
341	  along sheafification, via the localized-monoidal / reflective-localization
342	  template for sheaf categories. That principled construction is deferred: it is
343	  gated on closedness of the monoidal category of presheaves of modules (a
344	  monoidal-closed structure on \(\PshMod\)), which is not available at the pinned
345	  commit. The bespoke line-bundle construction above sidesteps that gap and is
346	  all the section graded ring requires.
347	\end{proof}

349	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
350	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
351	
352	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
353	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
354	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
355	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
356	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
357	global sections.
358	
359	\begin{definition}[Section multiplication]
360	  \label{def:sectionMul}
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
362	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
363	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
364	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
365	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
366	  \[
367	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
368	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
369	    \;\longrightarrow\;
370	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
371	  \]
372	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
373	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
374	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
375	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
376	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
377	  the sheafification unit
378	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
379	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
380	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
381	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
382	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
383	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
384	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
385	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
386	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
387	\end{definition}
388	
389	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
390	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
392	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
393	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
394	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
395	  the two composites
396	  \[
397	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
398	    \longrightarrow
399	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
400	    \cong
401	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
402	  \]
403	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
404	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
405	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
406	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
407	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
408	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
409	\end{lemma}
410	
411	\begin{proof}
412	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
413	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
414	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
415	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
416	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
417	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
418	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
419	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
420	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
421	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
422	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
423	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
424	  section multiplications.
425	\end{proof}

428	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
429	
430	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
431	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
432	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
433	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
434	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.

436	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
437	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
438	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
439	  \mathlibok
440	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
441	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
442	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
443	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
444	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
445	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
446	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
447	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
448	  \(1 \in A_0\).

451	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
452	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
453	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
454	  \mathlibok
455	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}

458	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
459	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying

1121	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is

1123	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.

1179	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1180	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1181	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1182	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1183	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.

1185	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1186	[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1187	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1188	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1190	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1191	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1192	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1193	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1194	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1195	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1196	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1197	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).

1199	\begin{proof}

1201	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1202	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1203	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1204	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1205	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1206	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1207	\end{proof}

1504	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1505	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1506	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type

1509	restricted sheaf over the base open. Neither is supplied directly by Mathlib: Mathlib
1510	does not provide the affine-local identification of \(\F|_W\) with the associated sheaf
1511	of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) together with the finiteness this transports.
